skill.name: azure-diagnostics
skill.description: Diagnose Azure production issues using Container Apps troubleshooting guides, Azure Resource Graph queries, KQL examples, resource health checks, and log review workflows.
skill.summary: Troubleshoot Azure production issues with Container Apps diagnostics, KQL log analysis, and resource health checks.
skill.author: microsoft

security_audit.summary: Static command-execution, network, environment-access, and weak-cryptography detections were reviewed in context. Most static hits are Markdown examples for Azure CLI, KQL, MCP tool calls, URLs, or app port configuration, so they are false positives for executable malware; however, the skill contains authority-style instruction text and operationally mutating Azure commands that create marketplace risk.

high_findings.title: Prompt Injection Attempt Detected
high_findings.description: SKILL.md uses authority-style language: "AUTHORITATIVE GUIDANCE — MANDATORY COMPLIANCE" and says the document is the official source. This can pressure an AI agent to treat marketplace content as higher priority than user or system instructions, even though the text is scoped to Azure troubleshooting and does not directly request bypassing security review.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The wording matches the prompt-injection warning for special authority and mandatory compliance. Confidence is not higher because the text stays within diagnostic guidance and does not tell the model to ignore prior instructions.

medium_findings.title: Operational Cloud Commands Can Change Live Azure Resources
medium_findings.description: The command examples are documentation, not embedded executable code, but several remediation commands can change Container Apps settings, registry configuration, image tags, or running diagnostics if copied or executed by an agent. This is legitimate for an Azure diagnostics skill, yet it requires user confirmation and careful subscription scoping.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The affected lines are visible Azure CLI or Docker examples with real operational impact. Confidence is medium-high because the commands are examples, not code that runs automatically.

medium_findings.title: Diagnostic Queries May Surface Sensitive Operational Data
medium_findings.description: The KQL and log-query examples are appropriate for troubleshooting, but they can reveal exception messages, stack traces, request names, dependency targets, and recent logs. This is a moderate data-handling risk when used in production tenants.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files explicitly query errors, stack traces, failed requests, dependency targets, and monitor logs. This is expected diagnostic behavior, but production log data can contain sensitive details.

low_findings.title: Static Network And Environment Findings Are Documentation Context
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to Microsoft Learn references and an example Container Apps health endpoint. The environment-access hit is a note to check whether the application reads process.env.PORT, not code that reads or exfiltrates environment variables.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Markdown examples or documentation links, with no executable source file and no outbound exfiltration logic. The evidence strongly supports a false positive for malware behavior.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detections Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak-cryptography static findings occur on lines that contain front matter, normal diagnostic wording, or KQL query text. No evidence found of MD5, SHA-1, custom cryptography, password hashing, or encryption code in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed context contains Azure diagnostic prose and KQL ordering clauses, not cryptographic implementation. Confidence is high because no matching crypto behavior is present at the reported lines.

dangerous_patterns.title: Markdown Shell Command Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill contains many bash, Docker, and Azure CLI snippets. They are not executed by the skill package, but an autonomous agent could run them against live infrastructure if the user authorizes commands too broadly.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command snippets are clearly present and operational. The risk depends on agent execution behavior, so this is a dangerous pattern rather than confirmed malicious code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Authority-Style Skill Instructions
dangerous_patterns.description: The phrase "AUTHORITATIVE GUIDANCE — MANDATORY COMPLIANCE" is an unsafe instruction style for marketplace skills because it can conflict with higher-priority runtime instructions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The phrase is directly present and matches a known prompt-injection pattern. It is less severe than an explicit ignore-instructions payload.

content.value_statement: Azure incidents are hard to triage when health, logs, revisions, and resource state are scattered across tools. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through focused Azure diagnostics for Container Apps, KQL logs, and Resource Graph checks.
actual_capabilities: Guides systematic Azure production issue diagnosis from symptoms to recent changes.
Provides Azure Container Apps troubleshooting steps for image pulls, cold starts, health probes, ports, and logs.
Supplies KQL examples for recent errors, failed requests, slow requests, and dependency failures.
Shows Azure Resource Graph query patterns for resource health, service incidents, and provisioning problems.
References Azure MCP tool workflows for AppLens, Monitor logs, and Resource Health checks.
Documents common Azure CLI commands for viewing resource health, activity logs, revisions, and container logs.
limitations: It does not deploy applications or create new Azure resources.
It assumes Azure CLI, MCP tools, or monitoring workspaces are already configured.
It focuses mainly on Azure Container Apps and general Azure health checks.
It provides guidance and examples, not guaranteed automated remediation.

use_cases.title: Triage a Container Apps outage
use_cases.description: Check revisions, registry configuration, health probes, ingress ports, logs, and recent activity during a production incident.

use_cases.title: Investigate application errors
use_cases.description: Use KQL examples to review exceptions, failed requests, slow requests, and dependency failures in Azure Monitor.

use_cases.title: Assess Azure resource health
use_cases.description: Use Resource Graph and Resource Health workflows to find degraded resources, active service incidents, and failed provisioning states.

prompt_templates.title: Check a failing app
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me diagnose why my Azure Container App is failing. Start with resource health, revisions, logs, and common configuration issues.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze recent errors
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a focused KQL investigation for recent application errors, failed requests, slow requests, and dependency failures in Azure Monitor.

prompt_templates.title: Find unhealthy resources
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Azure Resource Graph query patterns to identify unhealthy, degraded, or failed resources across the selected subscription scope.

prompt_templates.title: Run an incident diagnosis
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a step-by-step Azure incident diagnosis plan that correlates resource health, activity logs, Container Apps revisions, KQL findings, and recent changes.

output_examples.input: My Container App returns 503 after deployment.
output: Checks resource health and recent activity first.
Reviews revision status, ingress target port, and health probe behavior.
Suggests log queries and likely causes such as port mismatch or failing startup checks.

output_examples.input: I need to understand whether this is an Azure platform issue.
output: Looks at Resource Health and active service incidents.
Uses Resource Graph patterns to find degraded resources.
Separates platform health signals from application-level failures.

output_examples.input: Show me how to investigate recent API failures.
output: Focuses the time window for KQL analysis.
Reviews failed requests, exceptions, slow operations, and dependency failures.
Summarizes likely root causes and next diagnostic steps.

best_practices: Confirm the target subscription, resource group, and resource name before running any command.
Start with read-only health, activity, log, and query checks before changing configuration.
Redact secrets, customer data, and sensitive stack traces before sharing diagnostic output.
anti_patterns: Do not run remediation commands against production without explicit approval.
Do not skip resource health checks and start only from application logs.
Do not paste full production logs into external systems without reviewing sensitive data.

faq.question: What Azure services does this skill cover best?
faq.answer: It is strongest for Azure Container Apps, Azure Monitor logs, Resource Health, and Azure Resource Graph diagnostics.

faq.question: Can it deploy or create Azure resources?
faq.answer: No. The skill is for diagnostics and troubleshooting, not deployment or resource creation.

faq.question: Does it require Azure CLI access?
faq.answer: Some workflows use Azure CLI examples. MCP tools or portal-based checks may also be used when available.

faq.question: Can it change production settings?
faq.answer: The files include remediation examples that can change settings. Review every command and get approval before execution.

faq.question: Does it include KQL queries?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes KQL patterns for errors, failed requests, slow requests, and dependency failures.

faq.question: Is it safe to share diagnostic output?
faq.answer: Review output first. Logs and stack traces can contain secrets, customer data, host names, and internal service details.
